concepts.title: Circumcircle
concepts.conceptBody:
Circumcircle 

The circle passing through the vertices of the triangle $\text{ABC}$ is called the circum-

circle. Its radius $R$ is called the circum-radius. In the triangle $ABC$,

 

$\begin{array}{l} {} & \text{R}=\frac{\text{a}}{2\text{sinA}}=\frac{\text{b}}{2\text{sinB}}=\frac{\text{c}}{2\text{sinC}} \\ {} & \text{R}=\frac{\text{abc}}{4\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }} \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Incircle
concepts.conceptBody:
In-circle 

The circle touching the three sides of the triangle internally is called the inscribed or the in-circle of the triangle. Its radius $\text{r}$ is called the in-radius of the circle. In the triangle ABC

 

$\begin{array}{l} {} & \text{r}=\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }}{\text{s}} \\ {} & \text{r}=\left( \text{s}-\text{a} \right)\text{tan}\frac{\text{A}}{2}=\left( \text{s}-\text{b} \right)\text{tan}\frac{\text{B}}{2}=\left( \text{s}-\text{c} \right)\text{tan}\frac{\text{C}}{2} \\ {} & \text{r}=4\text{Rsin}\frac{\text{A}}{2}\text{sin}\frac{\text{B}}{2}\text{sin}\frac{\text{C}}{2} \\ {} & \text{r}=\frac{\text{asin}\frac{\text{B}}{2}\text{sin}\frac{\text{C}}{2}}{\text{cos}\frac{\text{A}}{2}}=\frac{\text{bsin}\frac{\text{A}}{2}\text{sin}\frac{\text{C}}{2}}{\text{cos}\frac{\text{B}}{2}}=\frac{\text{csin}\frac{\text{A}}{2}\text{sin}\frac{\text{B}}{2}}{\text{cos}\frac{\text{C}}{2}} \\ \end{array}$

Remark : From $r=4R\text{sin}\frac{A}{2}\text{sin}\frac{B}{2}\text{sin}\frac{C}{2}$, we find that $r\le 4R\cdot \frac{1}{8}$

$\Rightarrow 2\text{r}\le \text{R}\text{. }\text{ Here }\text{ equality }\text{ holds }\text{ for }\text{ the }\text{ equilateral }\text{ triangle}\text{. }\text{ }$

concepts.title: Excircle
concepts.conceptBody:
Escribed circles 

The circle touching $\text{BC}$ and the two sides $\text{AB}$ and $\text{AC}$ produced of $\vartriangle \text{ABC}$ externally is called the escribed circle opposite A. Its radius is denoted by ${{r}_{1}}$. Similarly ${{r}_{2}}$ and ${{r}_{3}}$ denote the radii of the escribed circles opposite angles $\text{B}$ and $\text{C}$ respectively.

 

${{\text{r}}_{1}},{{\text{r}}_{2}},{{\text{r}}_{3}}$ are called the ex-radii of $\vartriangle \text{ABC}$. Here

${{r}_{1}}=\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }}{s-a}=\text{stan}\frac{A}{2}=4R\text{sin}\frac{A}{2}\text{cos}\frac{B}{2}\text{cos}\frac{C}{2}$,

${{\text{r}}_{2}}=\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }}{\text{s}-\text{b}}=\text{stan}\frac{\text{B}}{2}=4\text{Rsin}\frac{\text{B}}{2}\text{cos}\frac{\text{C}}{2}\text{cos}\frac{\text{A}}{2}$,

${{\text{r}}_{3}}=\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }}{\text{s}-\text{c}}=\text{stan}\frac{\text{C}}{2}=4\text{Rsin}\frac{\text{C}}{2}\text{cos}\frac{\text{A}}{2}\text{cos}\frac{\text{B}}{2}$,

${{r}_{1}}{{r}_{2}}+{{r}_{2}}{{r}_{3}}+{{r}_{3}}{{r}_{1}}=4R+r$

${{r}_{1}}{{r}_{2}}+{{r}_{2}}{{r}_{3}}+{{r}_{3}}{{r}_{1}}={{s}^{2}}=\frac{{{r}_{1}}{{r}_{2}}{{r}_{3}}}{r}$

concepts.title: Distance of Orthocentre from Vertices of Triangle
concepts.conceptBody:
Distance of orthocentre from vertices of triangle

$\text{AD},\text{BE}$ are altitudes and $\text{H}$ is the orthocentre of a triangle $\vartriangle \text{ABC}$ as shown. As quadrilateral

 

CEHD is cyclic, (as opposite angles are supplementary)

angle $\text{EHA}=$ angle $\text{C}$

From $\vartriangle \text{AHE},\text{AHsinC}=\text{AE}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ AHsinC}=\text{ABcosA}=\text{AE}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ AH}=\frac{\text{ccosA}}{\text{sinC}}=\left( \frac{\text{c}}{\text{sinC}} \right)\text{cosA}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ AH}=2\text{RcosA}$

$\Rightarrow $ distances of orthocentre $\left( \text{H} \right)$ from the vertices $\text{A},\text{B}~\And~ \text{C}$ are :

$2\mathbf{R} \cos \mathbf{A},2\mathbf{R} \cos \mathbf{B} ~\And~ 2\mathbf{R} \cos C$ respectively.

Note : The triangle formed by joining the points $\text{D},\text{E }\And \text{ F}$ is known as pedal triangle.

concepts.title: Distance of Orthocentre from Sides of Triangle
concepts.conceptBody:
Distance of orthocentre from sides of triangle

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{DH}  =\text{AD}-\text{AH}  \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ DH}=\text{ABsinB}-2\text{RcosA} \\ \end{array}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ DH}=\text{csinB}-2\text{RcosA}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ DH}=2\text{RsinCsinB}+2\text{Rcos}\left( \text{B}+\text{C} \right)$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ DH}=2\text{RcosBcosC}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ The distances of orthocentre $\left( \text{H} \right)$ from the sides $\text{BC},\text{CA}\And \text{AB}$ are :

$2\text{RcosBcosC},2\text{RcosCcosA}$ and $2\text{RcosAcosB}$ respectively.

concepts.title: Important Theorems
concepts.conceptBody:
Important Theorem 

The centroid $\left( G \right)$, circumcentre $\left( O \right)\And $ orthocentre $\left( H \right)$ in any triangle are collinear. 

The centroid divides the line joining orthocentre and circumcentre in $2:1$ internally.

i.e. $\frac{\mathbf{OG}}{\mathbf{GH}}=\frac{1}{2}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ OG}=\frac{1}{3}\text{OH}$

 

$\And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ HG}=\frac{2}{3}\text{OH}$

The lengths of median 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{AD}=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{{{\text{b}}^{2}}+{{\text{c}}^{2}}+2\text{bcccosA}} \\ {} & \text{BE}=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{{{\text{c}}^{2}}+{{\text{a}}^{2}}2\text{acosB}} \\ {} & \text{CF}=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{{{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}2\text{abcosC}} \\ \end{array}$

Distance between orthocentre $\left( H \right)\And $ circumcentre $\left( 0 \right)$ 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{OH}=\text{R}\sqrt{1-8\text{cosAcosBcosC}} \\ {} & \text{OG}=\frac{1}{3}\text{OH}=\frac{\text{R}}{3}\sqrt{1-8\text{cosAcosBcosC}} \\ \end{array}$

Distance between circumcentre (O) & in-centre (I) / ex-centre 

 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{OI}=\text{R}\sqrt{1-8\text{sin}\frac{\text{A}}{2}\text{sin}\frac{\text{B}}{2}\text{sin}\frac{\text{C}}{2}} \\ {} & \text{O}{{\text{I}}_{1}}=\text{R}\sqrt{1+8\text{sin}\frac{\text{A}}{2}\text{cos}\frac{\text{B}}{2}\text{cos}\frac{\text{C}}{2}} \\ {} & \text{O}{{\text{I}}_{2}}=\text{R}\sqrt{1+8\text{cos}\frac{\text{A}}{2}\text{sin}\frac{\text{B}}{2}\text{cos}\frac{\text{C}}{2}} \\ {} & \text{O}{{\text{I}}_{3}}=\text{R}\sqrt{1+8\text{cos}\frac{\text{A}}{2}\text{cos}\frac{\text{B}}{2}\text{sin}\frac{\text{C}}{2}} \\ \end{array}$

Length of angle bisector 

$\text{AD}=\frac{2\text{bc}}{\text{b}+\text{c}}\text{cos}\frac{\text{A}}{2}$

concepts.title: When all Three Sides are Given
concepts.conceptBody:
When all three sides are given

To solve a triangle given the three sides $a,b,c$.

To determine angles $\text{A},\text{B}$ and $\text{C}$

The angles $\text{A},\text{B}$ and $\text{C}$ are determined by using the following relations :

$\begin{array}{l} {} & \text{tan}\frac{A}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{\left( s-b \right)\left( s-c \right)}{s\left( s-a \right)}} \\ {} & \text{sin}\frac{A}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{\left( s-b \right)\left( s-c \right)}{bc}} \\ {} & \text{cos}\frac{A}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{s\left( s-a \right)}{bc}} \\ \end{array}$

or by $\text{cos}$ formulas, $\text{cosA}=\frac{{{\text{b}}^{2}}+{{\text{c}}^{2}}-{{\text{a}}^{2}}}{2\text{bc}}$

Q) The sides of a triangle are 20, 30 and 21. Find the greatest angle. 

Solution : 

The greatest angle is opposite to the side whose length is 30 .

Let $\text{a}=20,\text{ b}=30,\text{c}=21$

We have to find angle $B$.

$\begin{array}{l} \text{sin}\frac{\text{B}}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{\left( \text{s}-\text{a} \right)\left( \text{s}-\text{c} \right)}{\text{ca}}}\left( \text{where }\text{ s}=\frac{\text{a}+\text{b}+\text{c}}{2} \right) \\ =\sqrt{\frac{\left( 35.5-20 \right)\left( 35.5-21 \right)}{20\times 21}} \\ =\sqrt{\frac{\left( 15.5\times 14.5 \right)}{20\times 21}}=\sqrt{5351} \\ =0.7315 \\ \text{sin}\frac{B}{2}=0.7315 \\ \frac{\text{B}}{2}={{47}^{\circ }}\text{ (Approximately) } \\ B=94 \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: When Two Sides and One Angle Between these sides are Given
concepts.conceptBody:
When two sides and the angle included between these are given

Q) If $\text{b}=251,\text{C}=147,\text{A}=47$, find the remaining angles. 

(Use Napier's rule) 

Solution : 

$\begin{array}{l} \text{tan}\frac{\text{B}-\text{C}}{2}=\frac{\text{b}-\text{c}}{\text{b}+\text{c}}\text{cot}\frac{\text{A}}{2} {} \\ \Rightarrow  \text{ tan}\frac{\text{B}-\text{C}}{2}  =\frac{251-147}{251+147}\text{cot}{{23}^{\circ }}{{30}^{'}} \\ {}  =\frac{26}{97}\text{tan}{{66}^{\circ }}{{30}^{'}}=0.268 2.2998  \text{ [we } \text{ have } \text{ used } \text{ these }]  \\ \end{array}$

$\Rightarrow  \text{ tan}\frac{\text{B}-\text{C}}{2}=0.6164$

$\Rightarrow  \frac{\text{B}-\text{C}}{2}={{31}^{\circ }}{{39}^{'}}$ (From the trigonometric tables)

$\Rightarrow  \text{ B}-\text{C}={{6318}^{'}}$

But $\text{B}+\text{C}=133 \left( 180-\text{A}=\text{B}+\text{C} \right)$

Adding $2 \text{ B}={{19618}^{'}}$

$\begin{align} \Rightarrow \,\,\angle B\,\,=\,98\,\,{9}' \\ \Rightarrow \,\,\angle C\,\,=\,34\,\,5{1}' \\ \end{align}$

concepts.title: When Two Angles and One Side are Given
concepts.conceptBody:
To solve a triangle having given two angles and a side

Let the given parts be denoted by B, C, a, then the third angle A can be found from the relation.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}}  {} & \text{A}=180-\left( \text{B}+\text{C} \right) \\ {} & \text{b}=\frac{\text{asinB}}{\text{sinA}} \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore \text{ logb}=\text{loga}+\text{logsinB}-\text{logsinA}$

Similarly, c can be found from the equation

$\text{logc}=\text{loga}+\text{logsinC}-\text{logsinA}$

Q) Solve the triangle $\text{ABC}$, given $\text{a}=18,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}=25,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}=108$ 

Suggested answer : 

$\begin{array}{l} {} & \text{C}=180-\left( 25-108 \right)=47 \\ {} &  \text{ b}=\frac{\text{asinB}}{\text{sinA}} \\ \end{array}$

Taking log on both sides, we get

$\begin{array}{l} \text{logb} & =\text{loga}+\text{log}\left( \text{sinB} \right)-\text{log}\left( \text{sinA} \right) \\ {} & =\text{log}18+\text{log}\left( \text{sin}108 \right)-\text{log}\left( \text{sin}25 \right) \\ {} & =\text{log}18+\text{log}\left( \text{sin}72 \right)-\text{log}\left( \text{sin}25 \right) \\ {} & =\text{log}18+\text{log}\left( 0.9511 \right)-\text{log}\left( 0.4226 \right) \\ \end{array}$

(from Trigonometric table)

$=1.2553+1.9782-1.6259$

$\begin{array}{l} {} & =1.2553-1+0.9782+1-0.6295 \\ \text{log}b & =1.076 \\ \end{array}$

Taking antilog, we get $\text{b}=40.52$.

Similarly using

$\text{logc}=\text{loga}+\text{log}\left( \text{sinC} \right)-\text{log}\left( \text{sinA} \right)\text{, }$

we can evaluate the value of $\text{C}$ as 31.23.

concepts.title: When Two Sides and Angle Opposite to One Side are Given
concepts.conceptBody:
Ambiguous Case 

Given two sides and the angle opposite to one of the sides 

Given the elements b, c and B of a triangle. This case is called Ambiguous case.

Draw the specified side $\text{AB}\left( =\text{c} \right)$ and make the angle $\text{B}\left( =\angle \text{ABD} \right)$ as given. Now to make the triangle mark an arc of radius equal to $\text{b}$ with centre at point $\text{A}$. This arc may intersect or touch or neither intersect nor touch the line BD. Accordingly we have the following results :

  • If $b<c\text{sin}B$, there is no triangle.

  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Ib}\,\,b=c\,\text{sin}\,\text{B}$ and $\text{B}$ is acute, there is one right angled triangle and right angle at $\text{C}$.

  • If $\text{b}>\text{csinB}$ and $\text{b}<\text{c}$ and $\text{B}$ is acute, there are two triangles satisfying the given conditions.

  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ If $b>c$, there is only one triangle.

  • If $\text{B}$ is obtuse, there is no triangle except when $\text{b}>\text{c}$.

Use sine rule to find angle $\text{C}$. Then $\text{A}=180-\left( \text{B}+\text{C} \right)$. The side a can also be found using sine rule.
